policy_statement: Provide additional funds for emergency food relief organizations to expand food donation.
mechanism_description: The state provides additional funds to emergency food relief organizations so they can expand food donation, reducing food waste sent for disposal and avoiding landfill methane emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Food surplus management (donation) → ↓ food waste landfilled → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Public funding increases capacity of food relief organizations (storage, refrigeration, transport, staffing, coordination) to collect and distribute edible surplus food.  
(2) Mediating process: Less food is discarded into the waste stream and landfilled, reducing anaerobic decomposition that produces methane (and potentially reducing waste collection/handling emissions).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane emissions from landfills (and, secondarily, reduced upstream emissions if donation displaces some new food production).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced methane emissions from landfills due to diverting food waste from disposal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Funding → more food donation → less food waste landfilled → less landfill methane → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it also improves resource efficiency (using food for its intended purpose), the mechanism description’s main climate lever is methane avoidance from reduced disposal, which is mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If increased donation does not actually reduce disposal (e.g., rebound in purchased food or weak diversion), climate benefits could be smaller; however the canonical mechanism explicitly includes “food waste reduction and landfill methane avoidance,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose mechanism is expanding food donation to reduce food waste sent for disposal will primarily act by avoiding landfill methane, fitting Mitigation consistently.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary subtype (e.g., upstream avoided production vs. disposal methane) is deferred to policy-level evidence and measured outcomes.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could partially function as Resource Efficiency (better utilization of edible food) and as a social policy; net GHG impact depends on whether donations truly displace disposal and on the baseline landfill gas capture rate, but dominant pathway remains methane avoidance from diversion.
key_indicators: “provide additional funds”; “financial assistance”; “emergency food relief organizations”; “expand food donation”
co_benefits: Improved food security; reduced hunger; social welfare support; potential reduced municipal solid waste management costs
instance_edge_case_notes: Although assigned to Mitigation, the text itself is primarily social assistance; climate impact depends on whether increased donation measurably diverts food from landfills (and is not purely additional distribution). No performance/diversion requirement is specified.
